A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "High fever and a sore on my arm that won't heal since I went rabbit hunting last week."

Vital Signs
BP 118/72
HR 96
RR 18
Temp 103.5°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Tularemia (Ulceroglandular) is the most likely diagnosis. Rabbit hunting + painless ulcer at handling site + painful lymphadenopathy + high fever = classic tularemia.
reasoning: Not asking about animal contact in patients with skin ulcers and lymphadenopathy
Assuming cellulitis when the classic painless ulcer + painful lymphadenopathy pattern is present
Missing the connection between rabbit/rodent handling and tularemia
Using beta-lactam antibiotics (ineffective against Francisella)

evidence.title: CDC Tularemia Guidelines
evidence.cite: Dennis DT, et al. JAMA. 2001;285:2763-2773
points: Streptomycin or gentamicin first-line
Ulceroglandular most common form
Rabbit/tick exposure history key

illnessScript.epidemiology: Tularemia (Francisella tularensis): 100-200 cases/year in US, endemic in south-central states. Transmission: tick/deerfly bite, handling infected animals (rabbits, rodents), contaminated water, inhalation. Hunters, veterinarians at risk. Bioterrorism agent.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Gram-negative intracellular pathogen → forms granulomas. Ulceroglandular (most common): cutaneous ulcer at inoculation site + regional lymphadenopathy. Other forms: glandular, oculoglandular, pneumonic (inhalation), typhoidal.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 118/72, HR 96, RR 18, Temp 103.5°F, SpO2 98% on RA; Left forearm: 1.5cm ulcer with raised, indurated borders and clean base. Minimal surrounding erythema. Not tender to palpation.; Left axillary lymph nodes massively enlarged (4-5cm), tender, warm, fluctuant. Epitrochlear nodes also enlarged on left.
illnessScript.workup: High clinical suspicion based on exposure + presentation. Serology (4-fold rise or single titer ≥1:160). Culture is hazardous (biosafety level 3) - notify lab! PCR available. CXR if pneumonic form suspected.
illnessScript.treatment: Streptomycin or gentamicin x10 days (first-line). Alternatives: doxycycline (higher relapse rate), ciprofloxacin. Lymph node suppuration may require drainage. Mortality <2% with treatment, 30-60% untreated pneumonic form.

distractors.explanation: Can also cause ulcer with black eschar from animal contact - different morphology though.
Ulcer + lymphatic spread, but usually gardeners/plant exposure, slower progression.
Animal exposure, but no cat contact. Different organism and morphology.
Would be more erythematous, not a clean ulcer. Wouldn't explain systemic illness severity.

vignette:
A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "My leg is turning black and I feel terrible. I ate raw oysters two days ago."

Vital Signs
BP 88/52
HR 125
RR 26
Temp 103.8°F
SpO₂ 94%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Vibrio vulnificus Septicemia/Necrotizing Fasciitis is the most likely diagnosis. Raw oysters + liver disease + hemorrhagic bullae + septic shock = Vibrio vulnificus. EMERGENCY.
reasoning: Not asking about raw shellfish consumption in patients with severe soft tissue infections
Not recognizing liver disease as major risk factor
Treating as simple cellulitis without recognizing necrotizing infection
Delaying surgical consultation - this is a surgical emergency

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Septic shock: antimicrobials within 1 hour of recognition (Strong)
Source control (surgical debridement) is critical for necrotizing fasciitis (Best Practice)
30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation within first 3 hours
Target MAP ≥65 mmHg; norepinephrine first-line vasopressor

illnessScript.epidemiology: 1.7 million cases/year in US, mortality 15-30%. Risk factors: extremes of age, immunosuppression, chronic disease, indwelling devices, recent surgery/hospitalization. Most common sources: pulmonary, urinary, abdominal, skin/soft tissue.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Dysregulated host response to infection → systemic inflammation → endothelial dysfunction → microcirculatory failure → tissue hypoperfusion → organ dysfunction. Lactate elevation reflects anaerobic metabolism from hypoperfusion.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 88/52, HR 125, RR 26, Temp 103.8°F, SpO2 94%; Right lower leg: tense edema, hemorrhagic bullae, dusky purple discoloration extending from ankle to knee. Areas of frank necrosis (black skin). Severe pain out of proportion. Crepitus palpable.; Acutely ill, diaphoretic, confused. Icteric sclerae (baseline from cirrhosis).
illnessScript.workup: Blood cultures x2 before antibiotics, lactate, CBC, BMP, LFTs, coagulation studies, procalcitonin. Source-directed imaging (CXR, CT, US). Calculate SOFA score for organ dysfunction.
illnessScript.treatment: Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures, lactate, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive/lactate ≥4. Vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) if MAP <65 despite fluids. Source control within 6-12 hours.

distractors.explanation: Can present similarly but oyster history points to Vibrio.
Rapid progression - check for crepitus and gas on imaging.
Bullae and rapid progression suggest necrotizing infection, not simple cellulitis.

vignette:
A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "I have a high fever and this weird rash on my hands and feet. I got bit by a rat at work last week."

Vital Signs
BP 115/72
HR 102
RR 18
Temp 103.2°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Rat-Bite Fever is the most likely diagnosis. Rat bite + fever + migratory polyarthritis + rash on hands/feet = classic RBF.
reasoning: Not asking about animal bites or exposures in patients with fever and rash
Missing that RBF can occur from rat scratches or handling without bite
Dismissing the rash as viral exanthem
Not recognizing the pattern: bite heals, then systemic illness develops

illnessScript.epidemiology: Psittacosis (Chlamydia psittaci): ~50 cases/year in US, likely underreported. Source: birds (parrots, parakeets, cockatiels, also poultry, pigeons). Transmission: inhalation of dried bird droppings/secretions. Pet store workers, poultry workers at risk.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Obligate intracellular bacterium → infects respiratory epithelium and macrophages → atypical pneumonia. Can cause severe pneumonia, hepatitis, myocarditis, encephalitis. Horder spots (pink blanching maculopapular rash) are rare but characteristic.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 115/72, HR 102, RR 18, Temp 103.2°F, SpO2 98%; Right index finger: healed puncture wound, no cellulitis, no drainage; Maculopapular and petechial rash on palms, soles, and extensor surfaces of arms. No mucous membrane lesions.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical suspicion with bird exposure + atypical pneumonia. Serology (microimmunofluorescence - 4-fold rise or single IgM ≥1:16). PCR on respiratory specimens. CXR: diffuse interstitial or lobar infiltrates.
illnessScript.treatment: Doxycycline 100mg BID x10-14 days. Alternative: azithromycin or fluoroquinolone. Beta-lactams ineffective (no cell wall target). Reportable disease - notify public health. Birds should be treated or isolated.

distractors.explanation: Rash on palms/soles requires syphilis consideration.
Rash and arthritis pattern, but bite history points elsewhere.
Fever + rash, but no tick exposure and different rash progression.

vignette:
A 34-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "The worst body aches of my life and high fever since I got back from Puerto Rico."

Vital Signs
BP 105/68
HR 96
RR 18
Temp 103.6°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Dengue Fever is the most likely diagnosis. Caribbean travel + fever + severe myalgias/arthralgias + rash + thrombocytopenia = dengue until proven otherwise.
reasoning: Not asking about recent travel in patients with fever and body aches
Missing dengue warning signs that predict severe disease
Treating with aspirin or NSAIDs (increases bleeding risk)
Not monitoring for the 'critical phase' around day 3-7 when fever breaks

illnessScript.epidemiology: Dengue is leading cause of fever in Caribbean/Latin America travelers. 50-100 million infections/year worldwide. Transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquito. Four serotypes - secondary infection increases severe dengue risk.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Flavivirus infects dendritic cells → viremia → immune activation → capillary leak, thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy. Severe dengue: plasma leakage (hemoconcentration, pleural effusion, ascites), hemorrhage, organ impairment. Defervescence is danger period.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 105/68 (low end), HR 96 (actually relatively slow for fever = relative bradycardia), RR 18, Temp 103.6°F; Diffuse maculopapular erythematous rash on trunk and extremities with scattered islands of sparing ('islands of white in sea of red'). Petechiae on lower extremities.; Positive: >20 petechiae per square inch in area under tourniquet
illnessScript.workup: Dengue NS1 antigen (days 1-5), IgM/IgG serology (after day 5). CBC: leukopenia, thrombocytopenia. Hematocrit rise indicates plasma leak. LFTs often elevated. Warning signs: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding.
illnessScript.treatment: Supportive care - no specific antiviral. Fluid management critical (judicious in capillary leak phase). Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin (bleeding risk). Monitor for warning signs → hospitalize. Severe dengue: ICU care, blood products PRN.

distractors.explanation: Same vector and geography - more joint-dominant. Can test concurrently.
Same region - usually milder. Test if pregnant or may become pregnant.
Can cause similar symptoms but travel history and rash point to dengue.

Vital Signs
BP 88/52

Temp 102.8°F
SpO₂ 95%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Cellulitis is the most likely diagnosis. Starting diagnosis but too severe for simple cellulitis.
reasoning: Not asking about saltwater exposure
Not recognizing cirrhosis as massive risk factor
Treating as simple cellulitis without surgery consult
Missing the bullous lesions as Vibrio clue

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Septic shock: antimicrobials within 1 hour (Strong) - doxycycline + 3rd-gen cephalosporin for Vibrio
Source control critical - surgical debridement for necrotizing fasciitis (Best Practice)
30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypoperfusion or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
Norepinephrine first-line; add vasopressin if inadequate MAP on norepinephrine

illnessScript.epidemiology: Vibrio vulnificus: found in warm coastal waters. Risk factors: liver disease (cirrhosis), immunocompromise, diabetes, hemochromatosis. High mortality (50%) especially with septicemia. Peaks in summer months.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Infection via wound exposure to seawater or ingestion of raw oysters. Produces cytotoxins and proteases causing tissue destruction. Rapidly progressive cellulitis with hemorrhagic bullae. Bacteremia leads to septic shock.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Massive edema L leg, hemorrhagic bullae, dusky skin, crepitus at wound. Spreading erythema with sharp demarcation.; Hypotensive, tachycardic, febrile. Ill-appearing.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis with appropriate exposure history. Blood and wound cultures (grows on routine media). Characteristic hemorrhagic bullae on physical exam. CBC, LFTs (underlying liver disease common).
illnessScript.treatment: Emergency: IV doxycycline PLUS third-generation cephalosporin OR fluoroquinolone. Early surgical debridement critical for wound infections. High mortality requires aggressive treatment. Prevention: avoid raw oysters if immunocompromised.

distractors.explanation: Gulf water + cirrhosis + bullae + rapid progression.
Rapid spread, bullae, sepsis - needs surgery.

stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Chikungunya is the most likely diagnosis. Similar presentation, same vector.
reasoning: Clinical presentation is classic for this diagnosis

illnessScript.epidemiology: Most common arboviral illness in travelers. Endemic in tropics/subtropics. Four serotypes - secondary heterologous infection increases risk of severe dengue. Aedes aegypti mosquito vector.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Flavivirus causes viremia, immune activation, capillary leak, thrombocytopenia. Severe dengue: plasma leakage causing shock, hemorrhage, organ impairment. Warning signs appear during defervescence.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Diffuse maculopapular rash sparing palms/soles. Petechiae on lower extremities.; Positive - >20 petechiae per square inch after BP cuff inflation.
illnessScript.workup: NS1 antigen (days 1-5), IgM/IgG serology (after day 5). CBC shows leukopenia, thrombocytopenia. Rising hematocrit indicates plasma leak. Monitor for warning signs: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding.
illnessScript.treatment: Supportive care - no specific antiviral. Judicious fluid management (avoid overload in leak phase). Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin. Monitor for warning signs requiring hospitalization. Severe dengue needs ICU care.

distractors.explanation: Colombia travel + fever + severe myalgias + thrombocytopenia + rash.
Must rule out - also endemic and more dangerous.
Usually milder.

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Enteric fever can progress to septic shock - early recognition essential
Septic shock: antimicrobials within 1 hour (Strong)
30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypoperfusion within 3 hours
Target MAP ≥65 mmHg with norepinephrine if needed

illnessScript.epidemiology: Salmonella typhi endemic in South Asia, Africa, Latin America. Transmission: fecal-oral via contaminated food/water. Risk: travelers to endemic areas without vaccination. Chronic carriers exist (gallbladder).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: S. typhi invades intestinal mucosa, survives in macrophages, disseminates via lymphatics and bloodstream. Sustained bacteremia causes fever. Rose spots (salmon-colored macules) pathognomonic. Complications: intestinal perforation, hemorrhage.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Temp 103.5°F but HR only 72 - RELATIVE BRADYCARDIA (pulse-temp dissociation); Hepatosplenomegaly. Mild diffuse tenderness.; Faint salmon-colored macules on trunk ('rose spots').
illnessScript.workup: Blood culture (gold standard, 60-80% sensitive in week 1). Bone marrow culture most sensitive. Stool culture less sensitive. Widal test (serology) has poor sensitivity/specificity. Leukopenia common.
illnessScript.treatment: Fluoroquinolone (if susceptible) or azithromycin or third-generation cephalosporin. Duration 10-14 days. Increasing resistance in South Asia. Dexamethasone for severe disease. Vaccination for travelers.

distractors.explanation: Pakistan VFR travel + prolonged fever + relative bradycardia + hepatosplenomegaly.
Must rule out - Pakistan has P. vivax.

vignette:
A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Non-healing ulcer on arm for 3 months."

Vital Signs
BP 122/78
HR 72
RR 14
Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Infected Wound is the most likely diagnosis. Common misdiagnosis.
reasoning: Clinical presentation is classic for this diagnosis

illnessScript.epidemiology: Transmitted by sandfly bite. Cutaneous most common in Americas and Mediterranean. Visceral (kala-azar) in Indian subcontinent, East Africa, Brazil. Risk: military, travelers to endemic areas.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Leishmania promastigotes inoculated by sandfly, convert to amastigotes, infect macrophages. Cutaneous: localized skin lesion. Visceral: disseminated infection of reticuloendothelial system (spleen, liver, bone marrow) causing hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: 2.5cm ulcer with raised, indurated borders ('volcano crater' appearance). Clean base, no purulence. Not tender. No lymphadenopathy.
illnessScript.workup: Cutaneous: skin biopsy/scraping for microscopy, PCR, culture. Visceral: bone marrow or splenic aspirate for amastigotes, rK39 rapid test (serology), PCR. Pancytopenia, elevated inflammatory markers.
illnessScript.treatment: Cutaneous: may self-heal, or topical paromomycin, intralesional antimonials, systemic for complex cases. Visceral: liposomal amphotericin B (first-line), miltefosine, sodium stibogluconate.

distractors.explanation: Afghanistan veteran + characteristic ulcer + sandfly endemic area.
Can look similar.
Often blamed incorrectly.

vignette:
A 65-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "Terrible stomach pain and trouble breathing - I'm getting worse fast."

History: He completed his third cycle of chemotherapy for lymphoma 10 days ago. He has been feeling progressively worse over the past 3 days with fevers, abdominal pain, and now respiratory distress.

Vital Signs
BP 85/50
HR 120
RR 28
Temp 102.4°F
SpO₂ 88% on 6L
Labs
WBC 0.8
ANC 200
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Neutropenic Sepsis is the most likely diagnosis. Post-chemo sepsis - but must find source.
reasoning: Clinical presentation is classic for this diagnosis

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Septic shock: antimicrobials within 1 hour of recognition (Strong)
Hyperinfection causes polymicrobial gram-negative bacteremia - broad coverage essential
30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation within first 3 hours
For high MDR risk: consider 2 gram-negative agents empirically (Weak)

illnessScript.epidemiology: Strongyloides stercoralis endemic in tropical/subtropical regions, Appalachian US. Unique autoinfection cycle allows decades-long infection. Hyperinfection in immunocompromised (especially corticosteroids, HTLV-1).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Larvae penetrate skin, migrate through lungs, mature in intestine. Autoinfection: larvae penetrate intestinal or perianal skin. Hyperinfection: massive larval dissemination in immunosuppression causing gram-negative sepsis.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Diffusely tender, distended. Decreased bowel sounds. Peritoneal signs.; Bilateral crackles, respiratory distress. CXR shows diffuse infiltrates.; Periumbilical purpura and petechiae. Linear urticarial tracks on trunk (larva currens).
illnessScript.workup: Stool O&P (multiple samples, low sensitivity). Strongyloides serology more sensitive. Eosinophilia (absent in hyperinfection). Sputum/BAL may show larvae in hyperinfection. Screen before immunosuppression if from endemic area.
illnessScript.treatment: Ivermectin (first-line): 200 mcg/kg daily x1-2 days. Hyperinfection: ivermectin until stool negative x2 weeks, plus broad-spectrum antibiotics for gram-negative sepsis. High mortality in hyperinfection.

distractors.explanation: Caribbean origin + immunosuppression + GI symptoms + gram-negative sepsis.
Different pattern.

vignette:
A 6-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "Fever, rash, and joint pain after rat exposure."

Vital Signs
BP 95/60
HR 110
RR 20
Temp 102.8°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Viral Illness is the most likely diagnosis. Common misdiagnosis.
reasoning: Clinical presentation is classic for this diagnosis

illnessScript.epidemiology: Rat-bite fever: Streptobacillus moniliformis (Americas) or Spirillum minus (Asia). Occupational risk: lab workers, pet store employees. Also from contaminated food/milk (Haverhill fever).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: S. moniliformis: gram-negative rod causing relapsing fever, polyarthritis, rash. S. minus: spiral organism causing ulcer at bite site, regional lymphadenopathy, relapsing fever. Both may cause endocarditis.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Maculopapular rash on extremities, including palms and soles. Petechiae.; Right knee swollen, warm, tender. Effusion present. Decreased ROM.; Healed bite wound on hand. No active infection at site.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis with exposure history. Blood cultures (fastidious, notify lab - needs special media). PCR available. Dark field microscopy for S. minus. No reliable serologic test.
illnessScript.treatment: Penicillin G IV x7-14 days, then oral amoxicillin. Alternative: doxycycline. Longer treatment (4-6 weeks) for endocarditis. Untreated mortality 10-15%.

distractors.explanation: Rat bite + fever + rash + arthritis.
Joint involvement requires consideration.

vignette:
A 55-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after being found collapsed at an outdoor job site. He is confused and febrile.

Vital Signs
BP 88/50
HR 130
RR 32
Temp 106.2°F
SpO₂ 96%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Heat Stroke is the most likely diagnosis. Core temp 106.2°F + AMS + hot dry skin + outdoor construction work during heat wave = exertional heat stroke.
reasoning: Waiting for labs before starting cooling - COOL FIRST
Not checking core (rectal) temperature
Assuming dry skin means dehydration not heat stroke
Not recognizing that medications can contribute

evidence.title: ACSM Heat Illness Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Casa DJ, et al. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2021;53:2283-2298
points: Cold water immersion for exertional heat stroke
Cool first, transport second
Core temp >40°C diagnostic

illnessScript.epidemiology: Trauma is leading cause of death ages 1-44. Trimodal death distribution: immediate (massive injury), early (hemorrhage, TBI), late (sepsis, MOF). Golden hour concept - early intervention improves survival.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Primary survey identifies immediate threats to life: Airway obstruction → hypoxia. Tension pneumothorax/tamponade → obstructive shock. Hemorrhage → hypovolemic shock. Traumatic brain injury → herniation. Lethal triad: hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Unresponsive to voice, withdraws to pain. GCS 8 (E2V2M4).; Core temp 106.2°F (rectal). BP 88/50, HR 130, RR 32.; Skin is HOT and DRY. No sweating despite high temperature.
illnessScript.workup: ABCDE approach. FAST exam for hemoperitoneum/hemopericardium. CXR, pelvis XR. CT pan-scan for stable patients. Labs: type and screen, CBC, BMP, coags, lactate, ABG. Massive transfusion protocol if indicated.
illnessScript.treatment: Airway: definitive airway if GCS ≤8 or airway compromise. Breathing: decompress tension PTX, chest tube for hemothorax. Circulation: hemorrhage control, permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) until surgical control, 1:1:1 transfusion ratio. Damage control surgery.

distractors.explanation: Diabetic patient - must check glucose immediately, but clinical picture fits heat stroke.
Sudden collapse could be cardiac - need EKG, but heat stroke explains everything.
Crossed the line to heat stroke with temp >104°F and altered mental status.
No witnessed seizure activity. Heat stroke explains AMS.

vignette:
A 3-year-old male is brought to the clinic by his parents, who report: "He's not talking like other kids his age and he's been very irritable."

Vital Signs
BP 95/60
HR 110
RR 22
Temp 98.8°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Lead Poisoning is the most likely diagnosis. Old house + pica + developmental delay + irritability + anemia = lead poisoning until proven otherwise.
reasoning: Attributing symptoms to 'normal toddler behavior'
Not asking about housing age and condition
Missing the connection between pica and lead exposure
Not testing siblings who share the same exposure

illnessScript.epidemiology: 500,000 US children ages 1-5 have elevated blood lead levels (≥5 μg/dL). Sources: lead paint in pre-1978 homes (dust, chips), contaminated water, occupational exposure (battery manufacturing, smelting), imported products, folk remedies.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Lead inhibits delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase and ferrochelatase → impaired heme synthesis → anemia. Competes with calcium → neurotoxicity (encephalopathy, developmental delay). Accumulates in bone (90% of body burden). Causes proximal tubular damage.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Irritable toddler, difficult to examine. Appears pale.; BP 95/60, HR 110, normal for age. Temp 98.8°F.; Says only 5-6 single words. Does not combine words. Follows simple commands. Good eye contact. No focal deficits.
illnessScript.workup: Blood lead level (BLL). Complete blood count (microcytic anemia, basophilic stippling). ZPP or FEP elevated. Abdominal XR if pica/paint chip ingestion suspected (radiopaque). Environmental assessment mandatory.
illnessScript.treatment: Remove from exposure source (most important). Chelation if BLL ≥45 μg/dL in children, ≥70-100 in adults, or symptomatic. Agents: succimer (DMSA) oral for mild, CaNa2EDTA + BAL for severe/encephalopathy. Nutritional support (iron, calcium, vitamin C).

distractors.explanation: Must rule out as cause of speech delay.
Need to rule out lead first. Social engagement appears normal per mother.
Need to identify and treat lead first.

vignette:
A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Feel really sick after spraying the fields - sweating, can't see right, stomach cramps."

Vital Signs
BP 100/60
HR 52
RR 24
Temp 98.2°F
SpO₂ 91%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Organophosphate Poisoning is the most likely diagnosis. Farmworker + recent pesticide spraying + SLUDGE symptoms + miosis + bradycardia = OP poisoning until proven otherwise.
reasoning: Not recognizing cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGE/DUMBELS)
Not asking about pesticide exposure in agricultural workers
Waiting for lab confirmation before treating
Inadequate atropine dosing - need much higher doses than typical

evidence.title: WHO Organophosphate Poisoning Guidelines
evidence.cite: WHO. Organophosphorus Insecticides. 1990 (updated)
points: Atropine until drying of secretions
Pralidoxime for nicotinic effects
Decontamination essential

illnessScript.epidemiology: Organophosphate poisoning: 3 million cases worldwide/year, 200,000+ deaths. Agricultural workers, pesticide applicators, children in farming communities at risk. Nerve agents (sarin, VX) are organophosphates - relevant for terrorism/military.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Irreversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase → acetylcholine accumulation at synapses. DUMBBBELS: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm/Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation. Nicotinic effects: fasciculations, weakness, paralysis. 'Aging' makes inhibition permanent.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Acutely ill, diaphoretic, drooling, clothes still wet with chemical odor; BP 100/60, HR 52 (BRADYCARDIA), RR 24, SpO2 91% on RA; Pupils 1mm bilaterally (PINPOINT MIOSIS), profuse lacrimation
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis - treatment should not wait. RBC cholinesterase (depressed) confirms exposure but takes time. Plasma pseudocholinesterase faster but less specific. Atropine challenge test: <2mg causes tachycardia in normal patients.
illnessScript.treatment: Decontamination: remove clothes, wash skin. Atropine: 2-4mg IV q5-10min until secretions dry (may need massive doses). Pralidoxime (2-PAM): reactivates cholinesterase if given before 'aging' (<24-48h). Benzodiazepines for seizures. Supportive care, ventilation.

distractors.explanation: Consider other cholinergic agents but pesticide exposure is clear.
Doesn't explain miosis, bradycardia, excessive secretions.

platform.title: Acute Illness with Exposure Case Discussion

vignette:
A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "The whole family has headaches - we thought it was the flu but it won't go away."

Vital Signs
BP 138/88
HR 102
RR 20
Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning is the most likely diagnosis. Multiple family members + headaches + winter + gas heating + symptoms better outside = CO poisoning until proven otherwise.
reasoning: Trusting pulse oximetry (reads falsely normal with CO)
Diagnosing 'viral syndrome' when multiple family members are sick
Not asking about home heating sources
Not recognizing that symptoms improve away from home

illnessScript.epidemiology: Carbon monoxide poisoning: 50,000 ED visits, 400+ deaths/year in US. Sources: faulty furnaces, generators indoors, car exhaust in closed garage, house fires. Winter months peak. Affects entire household simultaneously - key diagnostic clue.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: CO binds hemoglobin with 200-250x affinity of oxygen → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → left-shifted O2 dissociation curve → impaired oxygen delivery → cellular hypoxia. Brain and heart most vulnerable. Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome in 10-30%.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Alert but appears fatigued. Complains of persistent headache.; BP 138/88, HR 102, RR 20, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 98% on RA; Alert and oriented but slow to respond. Mild confusion. No focal deficits.
illnessScript.workup: COHb level (venous or arterial - normal <3% non-smokers, <10% smokers). Pulse oximetry UNRELIABLE (reads COHb as oxyhemoglobin). ABG shows normal PaO2 but decreased O2 content. ECG, troponin. CT/MRI if neurologic symptoms.
illnessScript.treatment: Remove from source, high-flow 100% O2 via NRB (half-life COHb 60-90 min on 100% O2 vs 4-5 hrs on room air). Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) if: loss of consciousness, COHb >25%, pregnancy, cardiac ischemia, severe neurologic symptoms. Prevention education.

distractors.explanation: Gas leak could explain symptoms and poses explosion risk.
Would not explain rapid improvement when leaving house.
Doesn't explain multiple family members simultaneously.

vignette:
A 42-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "Started vomiting after dinner on vacation and now I have bizarre tingling sensations."

Vital Signs
BP 108/68
HR 58
RR 16
Temp 98.2°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Ciguatera Poisoning is the most likely diagnosis. Caribbean vacation + reef fish (barracuda) + GI symptoms + temperature reversal + bradycardia = classic ciguatera.
reasoning: Diagnosing as 'food poisoning' without asking what fish was eaten
Not recognizing temperature reversal as pathognomonic
Ordering expensive neurological workups for peripheral neuropathy
Not warning about avoiding fish, alcohol, and nuts (can trigger recurrence)

evidence.title: CDC Ciguatera Guidelines
evidence.cite: CDC. Ciguatera Fish Poisoning. 2023
points: GI + neurological symptoms
Paradoxical temperature reversal pathognomonic
No specific antidote - supportive care

illnessScript.epidemiology: Dengue is leading cause of fever in Caribbean/Latin America travelers. 50-100 million infections/year worldwide. Transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquito. Four serotypes - secondary infection increases severe dengue risk.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Flavivirus infects dendritic cells → viremia → immune activation → capillary leak, thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy. Severe dengue: plasma leakage (hemoconcentration, pleural effusion, ascites), hemorrhage, organ impairment. Defervescence is danger period.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 108/68, HR 58 (BRADYCARDIA), Temp 98.2°F. Orthostatic changes present.; Decreased sensation to light touch in glove-stocking distribution. Cold stimulus perceived as burning.; Mild dehydration - dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor.
illnessScript.workup: Dengue NS1 antigen (days 1-5), IgM/IgG serology (after day 5). CBC: leukopenia, thrombocytopenia. Hematocrit rise indicates plasma leak. LFTs often elevated. Warning signs: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding.
illnessScript.treatment: Supportive care - no specific antiviral. Fluid management critical (judicious in capillary leak phase). Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin (bleeding risk). Monitor for warning signs → hospitalize. Severe dengue: ICU care, blood products PRN.

distractors.explanation: Scombroid causes flushing and histamine symptoms, not temperature reversal.
She ate fish not shellfish.

platform.title: GI and Neurological Symptoms Case Discussion

vignette:
A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "My shoulder is killing me and I feel weird after diving today."

Vital Signs
BP 128/82
HR 88
RR 16
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 97%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Decompression Sickness is the most likely diagnosis. Scuba diving + rapid ascent + joint pain + symptoms within 24h = DCS ('the bends') until proven otherwise.
reasoning: Not asking about recent diving in any patient with joint pain
Dismissing symptoms as 'musculoskeletal strain'
Not recognizing that symptoms can be delayed up to 24 hours
Failing to arrange hyperbaric treatment emergently

illnessScript.epidemiology: Decompression sickness (DCS): incidence 1-3 per 10,000 recreational dives. Risk factors: rapid ascent, repetitive dives, dehydration, older age, obesity, PFO (paradoxical embolism). Flying after diving increases risk (further pressure reduction).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Nitrogen dissolves in tissues under pressure → rapid ascent causes supersaturation → bubble formation. Type I (mild): musculoskeletal pain ('bends'), skin (marbling). Type II (serious): neurologic (spinal cord most common), pulmonary ('chokes'), vestibular.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 128/82, HR 88, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 97% on RA; No swelling, no ecchymosis, full ROM but painful. Deep tenderness not localizable to specific structure.; Mild difficulty with tandem gait. Subtle decreased sensation left arm. Otherwise intact.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis based on dive history and symptoms. Neurologic exam. No pathognomonic lab/imaging. Transthoracic echo with bubble study for PFO if recurrent DCS. Delay seeking care worsens outcomes.
illnessScript.treatment: 100% oxygen immediately (helps nitrogen washout). Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (recompression) is definitive treatment - do not delay for transport. US Navy Treatment Table 6 most common. Fluid resuscitation. Avoid air transport if possible (decreased cabin pressure).

distractors.explanation: If symptoms started immediately on surfacing, consider AGE - more emergent.
Timing with dive and character of pain point to DCS.
No trauma. Diving history is key.

platform.title: Joint Pain After Activity Case Discussion

vignette:
A 2-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by her parents. Her mother states: "She won't eat anything and keeps drooling. My husband found an empty battery case this morning."

Vital Signs
BP 90/55
HR 130
RR 28
Temp 99.0°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Esophageal Button Battery is the most likely diagnosis. Missing battery + refusal to eat + drooling = button battery in esophagus until proven otherwise. EMERGENCY.
reasoning: Waiting for symptoms before imaging when battery ingestion is suspected
Delaying removal - esophageal batteries must be removed within 2 hours
Not recognizing the 'double halo' sign on X-ray (pathognomonic for battery)
Confusing button battery with coin on X-ray

illnessScript.epidemiology: 13,000+ battery ingestions/year in US, most in children <6 years. Lithium 3V button batteries (20mm diameter) most dangerous. Esophageal impaction = surgical emergency. Death or severe complications from delayed diagnosis.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Battery lodged in esophagus → electrical current + hydroxide generation at negative pole → liquefactive necrosis begins in 15-30 minutes → full-thickness injury in 2 hours → can erode into aorta, trachea, or cause perforation. Most dangerous: new lithium batteries.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 90/55, HR 130 (tachycardic), RR 28, Temp 99.0°F, SpO2 98%; Fussy but consolable toddler. Actively drooling. Not in distress but uncomfortable.; Oropharynx clear, no battery visible. Pooling of saliva.
illnessScript.workup: Immediate X-ray (AP and lateral) - button batteries have characteristic 'halo' or 'double density' sign. Do NOT confuse with coin. Determine if esophagus vs stomach. Do NOT delay imaging for symptoms.
illnessScript.treatment: Esophageal battery: emergent endoscopic removal (do not wait for fasting). Honey (10mL q10min) or sucralfate may reduce injury during transport (not yet standard of care). Gastric battery: observe if asymptomatic and <20mm in child >5yr. Serial X-rays until passed.

distractors.explanation: Coins are common but have different X-ray appearance and less urgent timeline.
Wouldn't explain sudden refusal with drooling in otherwise healthy child.

A 34-year-old female presents to the emergency department, stating: "Can't breathe, throat feels tight."

Vital Signs
BP 82/50

stem: What is the FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis?

teaching.keyPoint: EPINEPHRINE IS THE ONLY FIRST-LINE TREATMENT. Give it IM in the lateral thigh immediately.
reasoning: Antihistamines are adjunctive - NOT first line.
Steroids prevent biphasic reaction but don't treat acute anaphylaxis.
May help bronchospasm but doesn't address systemic anaphylaxis.

evidence.title: WAO Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2020
evidence.cite: Cardona V, et al. World Allergy Organ J. 2020;13:100472
points: Epinephrine IM (0.01 mg/kg, max 0.5 mg adults) is FIRST-LINE
Administer epinephrine immediately - delays increase mortality
Position patient supine with legs elevated unless respiratory distress

illnessScript.epidemiology: Lifetime prevalence 1-2%. Common triggers: foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish), medications (antibiotics, NSAIDs), Hymenoptera venom, latex. Idiopathic in 20% of cases. Fatality rate ~0.3% of hospital-treated cases.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: IgE-mediated mast cell/basophil degranulation → massive histamine release + tryptase + prostaglandins + leukotrienes → vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, bronchospasm, mucus secretion → distributive shock, airway compromise.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis based on rapid onset multisystem involvement. Serum tryptase if diagnosis uncertain (peaks 1-2h, obtain within 4h). Post-event: referral for skin testing/specific IgE to identify trigger.
illnessScript.treatment: Epinephrine IM (0.3-0.5mg, 1:1000) FIRST - no absolute contraindications. Repeat q5-15min PRN. Adjuncts: IV fluids, H1/H2 blockers, steroids, bronchodilators. Observe 4-6h minimum (biphasic reactions in 5-20%).

distractors.explanation: Antihistamines are adjunctive - NOT first line.
Steroids prevent biphasic reaction but don't treat acute anaphylaxis.
May help bronchospasm but doesn't address systemic anaphylaxis.

stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Ischemic Stroke (Left MCA) is the most likely diagnosis. Sudden right-sided weakness and facial droop with aphasia. Classic left MCA territory.
reasoning: Not activating stroke alert immediately - delays door-to-needle time
Forgetting to check glucose STAT - hypoglycemia is a stroke mimic
Missing tPA window by spending too long on history before imaging
Lowering BP too aggressively - permissive hypertension maintains perfusion

illnessScript.epidemiology: 795,000 strokes/year in US; 87% ischemic. Risk factors: hypertension (#1), atrial fibrillation, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia, prior stroke/TIA. Large vessel atherosclerosis, cardioembolism, and small vessel disease are main etiologies.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Arterial occlusion → ischemic core (irreversible injury within minutes) + penumbra (salvageable tissue). Time-dependent neuronal death: 1.9 million neurons lost per minute of untreated large vessel occlusion. Collateral circulation determines penumbra size.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 178/98, HR 88 irregular, RR 16, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 97% RA. Fingerstick glucose 142 mg/dL.; Right lower facial droop. Forehead spares (wrinkles forehead symmetrically). illnessScript.treatment: IV alteplase within 4.5 hours (0.9mg/kg, max 90mg) if no contraindications. Mechanical thrombectomy for LVO within 24 hours if favorable imaging. BP management per guidelines. Admit to stroke unit.

distractors.explanation: MUST rule out before tPA. CT is essential.
Stroke mimic! ALWAYS check glucose immediately.
Post-seizure weakness. Would expect witnessed seizure.
Can cause focal deficits but usually has headache.

vignette:

HR 68
RR 14
Temp 98.2°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Parkinson's Disease is the most likely diagnosis. Asymmetric rest tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and shuffling gait in elderly male - classic PD presentation.
reasoning: Assuming all tremor is essential tremor - must distinguish rest vs. action tremor
Missing the bradykinesia and rigidity that distinguish PD from essential tremor
Not asking about medications that cause parkinsonism (metoclopramide, antipsychotics)
Forgetting to check for orthostatic hypotension - common in PD

evidence.title: AAN Parkinson Disease Guidelines 2019
evidence.cite: Zesiewicz TA, et al. Neurology. 2019;93:e2159-e2169
points: Carbidopa-levodopa most effective for motor symptoms
MAO-B inhibitors or dopamine agonists for early/mild disease
Dopamine agonists: higher risk of impulse control disorders
Deep brain stimulation for motor fluctuations refractory to medications

illnessScript.epidemiology: 10% lifetime risk of single seizure; epilepsy prevalence 1%. New-onset seizure causes: idiopathic (most common), structural (tumor, stroke, trauma), metabolic (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia), toxic (alcohol withdrawal, drugs), infectious (encephalitis).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Abnormal synchronized neuronal discharge → clinical manifestations depend on location and spread. Generalized: bilateral hemisphere involvement from onset. Focal: localized onset, may secondarily generalize. Status epilepticus: failure of seizure termination mechanisms.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 128/78 sitting, 108/68 standing (20 mmHg drop). HR 68 sitting, 72 standing. Orthostatic hypotension present.; Elderly male with masked facies (reduced facial expression). Quiet, monotone voice. Stooped posture.; 4-5 Hz rest tremor of right hand, 'pill-rolling' quality. Decreases with intentional movement. Tremor re-emerges when hands held outstretched (re-emergent tremor). Left hand has subtle rest tremor.
illnessScript.workup: Glucose (immediate), BMP, CBC, toxicology screen, anticonvulsant levels if applicable. Head CT if first seizure, focal features, prolonged postictal state, or not returning to baseline. MRI and EEG for epilepsy workup.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute seizure: protect airway, position safely, time seizure. Status epilepticus: benzodiazepine (lorazepam 4mg or midazolam 10mg IM), then fosphenytoin/levetiracetam. Address underlying cause. Driving restrictions per state law.

distractors.explanation: Must review medications - antipsychotics, metoclopramide, and others can cause parkinsonism.
If cognitive symptoms prominent or visual hallucinations present, consider DLB over PD.
ET causes action/postural tremor, not rest tremor. Usually bilateral, improves with alcohol.
Would have early falls, vertical gaze palsy, axial rigidity more than limb.

Vital Signs
BP 142/88
HR 78
RR 16
Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Chronic Arsenic Toxicity is the most likely diagnosis. Untested private well in Maine + neuropathy + Mees' lines + raindrop pigmentation + GI symptoms = classic chronic arsenic poisoning.
reasoning: Not asking about water source - especially private wells
Assuming municipal water is the source (it's regulated; private wells are not)
Missing Mees' lines on nail examination
Not recognizing 'raindrop' pigmentation pattern

evidence.title: ATSDR Arsenic Toxicological Profile
evidence.cite: ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. 2007
points: Skin changes, neuropathy, cancer risk
24h urine arsenic for diagnosis
Chelation for acute poisoning, remove exposure source

illnessScript.epidemiology: Arsenic contamination from natural geological sources or industrial pollution. Endemic areas: Bangladesh, West Bengal, parts of US. Chronic exposure from drinking water causes multisystem disease.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Arsenic inhibits cellular enzymes, causes oxidative stress. Chronic exposure: peripheral neuropathy (sensorimotor axonopathy), skin changes (hyperpigmentation, keratoses), increased cancer risk (skin, lung, bladder).
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Tired-appearing woman; Multiple hyperpigmented macules on trunk ('raindrop' pattern). Scattered hypopigmented macules. Palmar hyperkeratosis.; White horizontal bands on multiple fingernails (Mees' lines)
illnessScript.workup: 24-hour urine arsenic (most useful for recent exposure). Hair/nail arsenic for chronic exposure. Neuropathy workup: EMG/NCS shows axonal sensorimotor neuropathy. Test water source.
illnessScript.treatment: Remove from arsenic source (most important). Chelation (DMSA, DMPS) controversial, may help if recent high-dose exposure. Supportive care for neuropathy. Cancer surveillance long-term.

distractors.explanation: Should check, but Mees' lines and skin changes aren't explained by B12 deficiency.
No diabetes history; skin and nail changes suggest toxic etiology.
Lead causes different constellation - no Mees' lines typically.

stem: The patient moved to a rural property in Maine one year ago with a private well. What is the most important question about the well?

options.text: Has it been tested for arsenic?

teaching.keyPoint: Private wells are NOT regulated and can contain naturally-occurring arsenic, especially in New England where bedrock geology concentrates arsenic in groundwater.
reasoning: Depth matters less than testing.
Chlorination doesn't remove arsenic.
Age is less relevant than testing.

evidence.title: ATSDR Arsenic Toxicological Profile
evidence.cite: ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. 2007
points: Skin changes, neuropathy, cancer risk
24h urine arsenic for diagnosis
Chelation for acute poisoning, remove exposure source

illnessScript.epidemiology: Arsenic contamination from natural geological sources or industrial pollution. Endemic areas: Bangladesh, West Bengal, parts of US. Chronic exposure from drinking water causes multisystem disease.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Arsenic inhibits cellular enzymes, causes oxidative stress. Chronic exposure: peripheral neuropathy (sensorimotor axonopathy), skin changes (hyperpigmentation, keratoses), increased cancer risk (skin, lung, bladder).
illnessScript.workup: 24-hour urine arsenic (most useful for recent exposure). Hair/nail arsenic for chronic exposure. Neuropathy workup: EMG/NCS shows axonal sensorimotor neuropathy. Test water source.
illnessScript.treatment: Remove from arsenic source (most important). Chelation (DMSA, DMPS) controversial, may help if recent high-dose exposure. Supportive care for neuropathy. Cancer surveillance long-term.

distractors.explanation: Depth matters less than testing.
Chlorination doesn't remove arsenic.
Age is less relevant than testing.

stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Chronic Mercury Poisoning (Elemental) is the most likely diagnosis. Gold mining with mercury + intention tremor + gingivostomatitis + erethism = classic mercury poisoning.
reasoning: Not asking about occupational history in detail
Missing artisanal gold mining as major mercury source globally
Not recognizing gingivostomatitis as classic finding
Attributing psychiatric symptoms to primary mental illness

evidence.title: ATSDR Mercury Toxicological Profile
evidence.cite: ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Mercury. 2022
points: Elemental mercury causes neuropsychiatric symptoms
Spot urine mercury for diagnosis
Chelation therapy and source removal

illnessScript.epidemiology: 10% lifetime risk of single seizure; epilepsy prevalence 1%. New-onset seizure causes: idiopathic (most common), structural (tumor, stroke, trauma), metabolic (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia), toxic (alcohol withdrawal, drugs), infectious (encephalitis).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Abnormal synchronized neuronal discharge → clinical manifestations depend on location and spread. Generalized: bilateral hemisphere involvement from onset. Focal: localized onset, may secondarily generalize. Status epilepticus: failure of seizure termination mechanisms.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Anxious, emotionally labile. Laughed then cried during interview.; Gingival inflammation, bluish-gray discoloration at gingival margins. Loose teeth. Metallic odor.; Fine intention tremor of hands, worse with finger-to-nose testing
illnessScript.workup: Glucose (immediate), BMP, CBC, toxicology screen, anticonvulsant levels if applicable. Head CT if first seizure, focal features, prolonged postictal state, or not returning to baseline. MRI and EEG for epilepsy workup.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute seizure: protect airway, position safely, time seizure. Status epilepticus: benzodiazepine (lorazepam 4mg or midazolam 10mg IM), then fosphenytoin/levetiracetam. Address underlying cause. Driving restrictions per state law.

distractors.explanation: Should rule out but clear occupational exposure explains findings.
Intention tremor (not resting), personality changes, and occupational exposure point to mercury.
Gingivitis and erethism indicate toxic cause.

stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Manganese-Induced Parkinsonism is the most likely diagnosis. Welder + symmetric parkinsonism + dystonia + 'cock-walk' gait + behavioral changes = manganism until proven otherwise.
reasoning: Diagnosing idiopathic Parkinson's disease without asking about occupational exposures
Not recognizing that manganese-induced parkinsonism looks different from PD
Missing the distinctive 'cock-walk' gait pattern of manganism
Expecting good response to levodopa (it doesn't work well in manganism)

illnessScript.epidemiology: 10% lifetime risk of single seizure; epilepsy prevalence 1%. New-onset seizure causes: idiopathic (most common), structural (tumor, stroke, trauma), metabolic (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia), toxic (alcohol withdrawal, drugs), infectious (encephalitis).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Abnormal synchronized neuronal discharge → clinical manifestations depend on location and spread. Generalized: bilateral hemisphere involvement from onset. Focal: localized onset, may secondarily generalize. Status epilepticus: failure of seizure termination mechanisms.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Symmetric bradykinesia. Cogwheel rigidity in all extremities. Postural tremor greater than resting tremor. Dystonic posturing of hands.; Walks on toes with 'cock-walk' pattern. Decreased arm swing. Difficulty with turns. Retropulsion on pull test.; Masked facies. Reduced blink rate. Fixed 'grinning' expression.
illnessScript.workup: Glucose (immediate), BMP, CBC, toxicology screen, anticonvulsant levels if applicable. Head CT if first seizure, focal features, prolonged postictal state, or not returning to baseline. MRI and EEG for epilepsy workup.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute seizure: protect airway, position safely, time seizure. Status epilepticus: benzodiazepine (lorazepam 4mg or midazolam 10mg IM), then fosphenytoin/levetiracetam. Address underlying cause. Driving restrictions per state law.

distractors.explanation: Classic PD must be considered, but typically asymmetric at onset and responds to levodopa.
No history of dopamine-blocking medications.
Doesn't explain rigidity, bradykinesia, and gait abnormality.

platform.title: Tremor and Gait Problems Case Discussion

vignette:
A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "My tremor and stiffness have gotten worse, I'm having balance problems, and my Parkinson's medications don't seem to be working anymore."

Vital Signs
BP 138/82
HR 74
RR 14
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Parkinson's Disease is the most likely diagnosis. Similar appearance but different mechanism.
reasoning: Clinical presentation is classic for this diagnosis

illnessScript.epidemiology: Manganese neurotoxicity from occupational exposure: welding, mining, battery manufacturing, ferroalloy production. Also from contaminated well water. Risk increases with duration and intensity of exposure.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Manganese accumulates in basal ganglia (globus pallidus). Causes parkinsonism-like syndrome but distinct from Parkinson disease: earlier psychiatric symptoms (manganese madness), cock-walk gait, less tremor, no response to levodopa.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Bradykinesia, rigidity, rest tremor. Distinctive 'cock walk' - toe walking, high-stepping gait. No response to L-dopa trial.
illnessScript.workup: Occupational/environmental history essential. Blood/urine manganese (reflects recent exposure only). MRI: T1 hyperintensity in globus pallidus (characteristic). PET shows different pattern than PD.
illnessScript.treatment: Remove from exposure. Chelation (CaNa2EDTA) may help early cases. Levodopa typically ineffective (post-synaptic damage). Symptoms may progress even after exposure stops. Prevention: industrial hygiene.

distractors.explanation: Welder + parkinsonism + no L-dopa response.
No offending medications.

vignette:
A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "I've been having memory problems and my family says my personality has changed over the past two years."

Vital Signs
BP 128/78
HR 72
RR 14
Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Early-Onset Dementia is the most likely diagnosis. Must rule out but younger than typical.
reasoning: Clinical presentation is classic for this diagnosis

illnessScript.epidemiology: Organic solvent neurotoxicity from occupational exposure: painters, dry cleaners, petrochemical workers, printers. Solvents include toluene, trichloroethylene, n-hexane, carbon disulfide.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Lipophilic solvents cross blood-brain barrier. Acute: CNS depression, confusion, euphoria. Chronic: cognitive impairment (painters syndrome), peripheral neuropathy (n-hexane, carbon disulfide), cerebellar dysfunction.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Impaired short-term memory. Difficulty with concentration and processing speed. Affect flat. No focal deficits.
illnessScript.workup: Occupational history essential. Urinary metabolites for specific solvents. Neuropsychological testing for cognitive impairment. EMG/NCS for peripheral neuropathy. MRI may show white matter changes.
illnessScript.treatment: Remove from exposure. No specific antidote for most solvents. Supportive care. Cognitive rehabilitation. Neuropathy may be irreversible. Workers compensation evaluation. Prevention: engineering controls, PPE.

distractors.explanation: 20 years solvent exposure + cognitive decline + personality change.
Often misdiagnosed as this.

Temp 98.4°F

stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: TIA is the most likely diagnosis. If symptoms resolve - but still warrants urgent workup.
reasoning: Delaying CT to get 'better history' - IMAGE FIRST
Missing the last known well time for tPA eligibility
Not recognizing atypical stroke presentations
Forgetting to check glucose (hypoglycemia mimics stroke)

evidence.title: AHA/ASA Acute Ischemic Stroke Guidelines 2019
evidence.cite: Powers WJ, et al. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418
points: Door-to-needle time <60 minutes for IV alteplase (Class I)
IV alteplase within 4.5 hours of symptom onset for eligible patients
Mechanical thrombectomy within 24 hours for LVO with salvageable tissue
NIHSS assessment to quantify stroke severity

illnessScript.epidemiology: 795,000 strokes/year in US; 87% ischemic. Risk factors: hypertension (#1), atrial fibrillation, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia, prior stroke/TIA. Large vessel atherosclerosis, cardioembolism, and small vessel disease are main etiologies.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Arterial occlusion → ischemic core (irreversible injury within minutes) + penumbra (salvageable tissue). Time-dependent neuronal death: 1.9 million neurons lost per minute of untreated large vessel occlusion. Collateral circulation determines penumbra size.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 178/102, HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 96%. Finger stick glucose: 142 mg/dL.; Elderly male, awake and alert but appears frustrated trying to communicate. Right facial droop. Not in distress.; Expressive aphasia - Cannot name objects ('watch', 'pen'), non-fluent speech with effort, uses wrong words (paraphasias). Comprehension relatively intact - follows simple commands. Repetition impaired.
illnessScript.workup: Emergent non-contrast CT (rule out hemorrhage), glucose (exclude hypoglycemia mimic). CT angiography for LVO detection. Calculate NIHSS. Door-to-imaging <25 min goal.
illnessScript.treatment: IV alteplase within 4.5 hours (0.9mg/kg, max 90mg) if no contraindications. Mechanical thrombectomy for LVO within 24 hours if favorable imaging. BP management per guidelines. Admit to stroke unit.

distractors.explanation: Must rule out with CT before tPA - contraindicated.
Check glucose immediately - easily reversible.
Usually younger, history of similar events.

vignette:
A 65-year-old female presents to the emergency department, stating: "I had trouble speaking clearly earlier today, but it went away after about 20 minutes."

Vital Signs
BP 162/94
HR 78
RR 16
Temp 98.1°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: TIA (Carotid stenosis) is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: transient focal deficits (dysarthria, hemiparesis) that resolved, prior amaurosis fugax, carotid bruit, multiple vascular risk factors.
reasoning: Assuming resolved symptoms mean no workup is needed - TIA is a warning sign of imminent stroke
Missing the prior amaurosis fugax episode - this indicates carotid disease
Not calculating ABCD2 score to risk stratify
Not performing carotid imaging in patient with carotid bruit

evidence.title: AHA/ASA TIA Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Kleindorfer DO, et al. Stroke. 2021;52:e364-e467
points: ABCD2 score for risk stratification
Neuroimaging (MRI preferred) within 24 hours
Carotid imaging (CTA/MRA/duplex) for anterior circulation TIA
Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days if minor stroke/high-risk TIA

illnessScript.epidemiology: 240,000 TIAs/year in US. 10-15% stroke risk within 90 days, half within 48 hours. Risk factors same as stroke: HTN, DM, smoking, AFib, carotid stenosis. ABCD2 score predicts short-term stroke risk.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Transient arterial occlusion (embolism or thrombosis) → brief focal ischemia → symptoms resolve as clot lyses or collaterals open. No infarction on imaging. Warning sign of impending stroke - 'brain angina'.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 162/94 (elevated), HR 78 regular, RR 16, Temp 98.1°F, SpO2 98% RA; CURRENTLY NORMAL: Alert, oriented x3. Speech fluent with intact naming, repetition, comprehension. CN II-XII intact including visual fields. Strength 5/5 all extremities, symmetric. Sensation intact. Reflexes 2+ symmetric. Coordination intact. Gait normal.; RIGHT-SIDED CAROTID BRUIT heard. Left side clear.
illnessScript.workup: Urgent evaluation within 24-48h: MRI with DWI (30% have acute infarct despite symptom resolution), carotid imaging (US, CTA, or MRA), ECG and telemetry for AFib, TTE. Labs: glucose, lipids, CBC, BMP.
illnessScript.treatment: Antiplatelet: aspirin + clopidogrel x21 days (POINT, CHANCE trials) then single agent. Statin regardless of LDL. BP control. Carotid endarterectomy if 70-99% ipsilateral stenosis. Anticoagulation if AFib.

distractors.explanation: Even though symptoms resolved, must rule out actual infarction with MRI. 30% of 'TIAs' <24h have DWI changes.
Hemorrhage can occasionally present with transient symptoms. CT needed.
Seizure can cause transient focal deficits (Todd's paralysis), but no witnessed seizure activity.
Can cause transient neurological symptoms, but usually includes headache and visual aura.

stem: You hear a right carotid bruit on exam. What is the sensitivity of carotid bruit for significant stenosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Carotid bruit has only 20-30% sensitivity. Many patients with significant stenosis have no bruit, and some bruits are transmitted cardiac murmurs.
reasoning: Carotid bruit actually has poor sensitivity (20-30%) for detecting stenosis.
Carotid bruit is less sensitive than this.
It's not this uncommon - about 20-30% of patients with significant stenosis will have an audible bruit.

evidence.title: AHA/ASA TIA Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Kleindorfer DO, et al. Stroke. 2021;52:e364-e467
points: ABCD2 score for risk stratification
Neuroimaging (MRI preferred) within 24 hours
Carotid imaging (CTA/MRA/duplex) for anterior circulation TIA

illnessScript.epidemiology: 240,000 TIAs/year in US. 10-15% stroke risk within 90 days, half within 48 hours. Risk factors same as stroke: HTN, DM, smoking, AFib, carotid stenosis. ABCD2 score predicts short-term stroke risk.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Transient arterial occlusion (embolism or thrombosis) → brief focal ischemia → symptoms resolve as clot lyses or collaterals open. No infarction on imaging. Warning sign of impending stroke - 'brain angina'.
illnessScript.workup: Urgent evaluation within 24-48h: MRI with DWI (30% have acute infarct despite symptom resolution), carotid imaging (US, CTA, or MRA), ECG and telemetry for AFib, TTE. Labs: glucose, lipids, CBC, BMP.
illnessScript.treatment: Antiplatelet: aspirin + clopidogrel x21 days (POINT, CHANCE trials) then single agent. Statin regardless of LDL. BP control. Carotid endarterectomy if 70-99% ipsilateral stenosis. Anticoagulation if AFib.

distractors.explanation: Carotid bruit actually has poor sensitivity (20-30%) for detecting stenosis.
Carotid bruit is less sensitive than this.
It's not this uncommon - about 20-30% of patients with significant stenosis will have an audible bruit.

vignette:
A 68-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Sudden right-sided weakness and trouble speaking."

Vital Signs
BP 178/102
HR 88
RR 18
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 97%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Ischemic Stroke (MCA) is the most likely diagnosis. Sudden onset focal neuro deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia) + AFib + subtherapeutic INR = classic cardioembolic stroke.
reasoning: Waiting for all labs before CT - only glucose and coags needed before tPA decision
Treating blood pressure too aggressively - only treat if >185/110 for tPA, >220/120 otherwise
Missing the history of dietary vitamin K increase reducing warfarin efficacy
Not recognizing the prior 'clumsiness' as a TIA warning sign

illnessScript.epidemiology: AFib causes 15-20% of ischemic strokes. AFib-related strokes are larger, more disabling, and more fatal than other etiologies. Risk increases with CHA2DS2-VASc score. Prior stroke/TIA confers highest risk.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Atrial stasis (especially LAA) → thrombus formation → embolization to cerebral circulation → large vessel occlusion → MCA territory most common. Cardioembolic strokes have higher rates of hemorrhagic transformation.
illnessScript.workup: Acute stroke workup + prolonged cardiac monitoring (30-day event monitor or implantable loop recorder) if paroxysmal AFib suspected but not documented. TEE to evaluate LAA thrombus before cardioversion.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute: IV thrombolysis and/or thrombectomy per guidelines (anticoagulation is NOT contraindication to acute therapy). Secondary prevention: anticoagulation (DOAC preferred over warfarin) started 4-14 days post-stroke depending on size.

distractors.explanation: Must rule out with CT BEFORE any thrombolytics. Can present similarly.
Can cause focal neurological deficits that mimic stroke. Check glucose immediately.
Symptoms persist >60 min and NIHSS 18. By definition, this is now a stroke, not TIA.
Todd's paralysis can mimic stroke, but no seizure witnessed.

vignette:
A 65-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Worsening back pain with new leg weakness."

Vital Signs
BP 152/88
HR 92
RR 18
Temp 98.8°F
SpO₂ 95%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Malignant Spinal Cord Compression is the most likely diagnosis. Progressive back pain + weight loss + smoking history + leg weakness + bladder dysfunction = classic presentation. Lung cancer is #1 cause.
reasoning: Attributing back pain to 'degenerative disease' without considering red flags
Not asking about urinary symptoms - retention is a late but critical finding
Ordering lumbar MRI when symptoms localize to thoracic spine
Waiting for oncology consult before starting dexamethasone

evidence.title: ASCO Spinal Cord Compression Guidelines
evidence.cite: Defined standard of care for malignant cord compression
points: EMERGENCY - time to treatment determines neurologic outcome
High-dose dexamethasone immediately if suspected
MRI of entire spine within hours
Surgery if single-level compression, good prognosis, neurologically deteriorating

illnessScript.epidemiology: 795,000 strokes/year in US; 87% ischemic. Risk factors: hypertension (#1), atrial fibrillation, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia, prior stroke/TIA. Large vessel atherosclerosis, cardioembolism, and small vessel disease are main etiologies.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Arterial occlusion → ischemic core (irreversible injury within minutes) + penumbra (salvageable tissue). Time-dependent neuronal death: 1.9 million neurons lost per minute of untreated large vessel occlusion. Collateral circulation determines penumbra size.
illnessScript.workup: Emergent non-contrast CT (rule out hemorrhage), glucose (exclude hypoglycemia mimic). CT angiography for LVO detection. Calculate NIHSS. Door-to-imaging <25 min goal.
illnessScript.treatment: IV alteplase within 4.5 hours (0.9mg/kg, max 90mg) if no contraindications. Mechanical thrombectomy for LVO within 24 hours if favorable imaging. BP management per guidelines. Admit to stroke unit.

distractors.explanation: Can present similarly. Usually has fever and elevated WBC. Surgical emergency.
Consider if on anticoagulation or post-procedure. Usually more acute.
Could cause pain but not weight loss or progressive neuro deficits.
Could be pathologic fracture from mets. Would show on imaging.

stem: The patient has 3/5 strength bilaterally with bilateral Babinski signs. What does this pattern indicate?

options.text: Upper motor neuron lesion above the conus medullaris

reasoning: LMN lesions cause hyporeflexia and downgoing toes. Babinski is an UMN sign.

Babinski is an involuntary reflex and cannot be 'faked.'

vignette:
A 16-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Sudden severe pain in my left testicle that woke me up."

Vital Signs
BP 138/82
HR 102
RR 18
Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Testicular Torsion is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: sudden severe pain, high-riding testicle with horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex, adolescent male.
reasoning: Waiting for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high - go to OR
Assuming epididymitis in an adolescent (torsion is more common in this age group)
Not recognizing intermittent torsion (pain that resolved spontaneously)
Forgetting that cremasteric reflex ABSENT = highly suggestive of torsion

illnessScript.epidemiology: Peak incidence in adolescents (12-18 years) and neonates. Annual incidence 1 in 4000 males <25. Left side slightly more common. Bell-clapper deformity (horizontal lie) is predisposing anatomic variant.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Spermatic cord twists → venous congestion (early) → arterial compromise (late) → testicular ischemia → necrosis. Salvage rates: >90% if <6 hours, 50% at 12 hours, <10% after 24 hours. Time to detorsion is critical.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 138/82, HR 102, RR 18, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 99%; Adolescent male in obvious distress, lying still, reluctant to move. Appears pale and diaphoretic.; Soft, mild tenderness in left lower quadrant. No rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds present.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis - do not delay surgery for imaging if high suspicion. Doppler ultrasound if diagnosis uncertain (sensitivity 88-100%). Absent or decreased flow is diagnostic. Cremasteric reflex typically absent.
illnessScript.treatment: Surgical emergency - manual detorsion may be attempted while awaiting OR (lateral 'open book' rotation). Bilateral orchiopexy at surgery (contralateral bell-clapper common). Orchiectomy if non-viable.

distractors.explanation: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Salvage rate: <6hrs = 90%, 12-24hrs = 20-50%, >24hrs = <10%. Time is testicle!
Can mimic torsion but is benign. 'Blue dot sign' at upper pole is pathognomonic. Managed conservatively but if uncertain, explore.
Less common in adolescents than adults. Usually more gradual onset. Cremasteric reflex preserved. Positive Prehn sign (relief with elevation).
Often associated with mumps or epididymitis. More gradual onset.

vignette:
A 72-year-old female presents to the emergency department, stating: "My mother is confused and acting strange."

Vital Signs
BP 128/76
HR 78
RR 16
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 96%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH) is the most likely diagnosis. Euvolemic, concentrated urine, high urine sodium, low serum osmolality. Classic pattern.
reasoning: Correcting sodium too fast (>8-10 mEq/L in 24h) → osmotic demyelination syndrome
Not determining the volume status (hypovolemic vs euvolemic vs hypervolemic)
Giving normal saline when the problem is SIADH (can worsen hyponatremia!)
Not checking urine studies (urine Na, urine osmolality)

illnessScript.epidemiology: Most common electrolyte disorder; present in 15-30% of hospitalized patients. Risk factors: age, medications (thiazides, SSRIs, carbamazepine), heart failure, cirrhosis, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, malignancy.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Serum Na reflects water balance, not sodium balance. Hypotonic hyponatremia: water excess relative to sodium. ADH-mediated water retention (SIADH), decreased effective circulating volume (CHF, cirrhosis), or primary polydipsia. Brain edema risk if acute.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 128/76, HR 78, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 96% on RA; Elderly female, appears confused but arousable. Thin. No acute distress.; Alert but disoriented to date and place. Difficulty following commands. Word-finding difficulty. Asterixis present.
illnessScript.workup: Serum osmolality (confirm hypotonic), urine osmolality and sodium (assess renal response), volume status assessment. If euvolemic hypotonic: TSH, cortisol. Consider CT head if neurologic symptoms.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute symptomatic: 3% saline 100-150mL bolus, may repeat. Correction rate ≤8 mEq/L per 24h to avoid osmotic demyelination. Chronic: treat underlying cause, fluid restriction for SIADH, salt tablets/loop diuretics if needed.

distractors.explanation: SIADH is commonly paraneoplastic - small cell lung cancer is classic. Must image chest.
CNS lesions (tumor, bleed, infection, trauma) can cause SIADH. Consider head imaging.
Many drugs cause SIADH (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide). Review medications.
Can cause hyponatremia. Check TSH. Usually less severe.

vignette:

Vital Signs
BP 128/76
HR 78
RR 16
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 96%
stem: A patient has sodium 118, serum osmolality 248 (low), urine osmolality 520 (inappropriately concentrated), urine sodium 65 (high), and is clinically euvolemic. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Classic SIADH: Euvolemic, low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine (>100), HIGH urine sodium (>40). Kidneys can excrete sodium but not water.
reasoning: Hypovolemia would have low urine sodium (<20) as kidneys retain sodium. This patient has high urine sodium (65).
Polydipsia causes hyponatremia but urine should be MAXIMALLY DILUTE (<100 mOsm). This patient has concentrated urine (520).
Heart failure is hypervolemic hyponatremia with edema. This patient is euvolemic.

vignette:
A 34-year-old transgender female presents to the clinic, stating: "Severe pain in my right side that came on suddenly."

Vital Signs
BP 148/92
HR 98
RR 18
Temp 98.8°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stone) is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: sudden severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin with hematuria. Note: Patient has male anatomy despite identifying as female.
reasoning: Assuming all flank pain is kidney stones without considering AAA in appropriate patients
Missing fever which suggests infected stone requiring urgent intervention
Not checking for hydronephrosis which may require urgent urology consultation
Forgetting to strain urine to capture stone for analysis

evidence.title: AUA/Endourology Society Kidney Stones Guidelines 2022
evidence.cite: Assimos D, et al. J Urol. 2022;207(1):8-15
points: Low-dose non-contrast CT is imaging modality of choice
Alpha-blockers (medical expulsive therapy) for distal ureteral stones <10mm
NSAIDs first-line for acute renal colic
Intervention for stones >10mm, persistent obstruction, or infection

illnessScript.epidemiology: Lifetime prevalence 10-15%. Peak age 20-50, M>F. 80% calcium stones (oxalate > phosphate), 10% uric acid, 10% struvite/cystine. Recurrence 50% within 5-10 years. Risk: low fluid intake, high sodium/protein diet, obesity.

stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: CKD Stage 4 is the most likely diagnosis. GFR 22 - must find cause.

Missing that HTN is secondary, not cause

illnessScript.epidemiology: Chronic kidney disease of unknown etiology (CKDu) in agricultural communities: Sri Lanka, Central America, India. Affects young male agricultural workers. Not explained by traditional CKD risk factors.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Etiology unclear: heat stress/dehydration, nephrotoxic agrochemicals, heavy metals, infections proposed. Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis pattern. Slowly progressive to ESRD.
illnessScript.workup: Elevated creatinine, reduced eGFR in absence of diabetes, hypertension, glomerulonephritis. Bland urinary sediment (minimal proteinuria). Renal biopsy shows tubulointerstitial nephritis.
illnessScript.treatment: No specific treatment. Supportive CKD management. Hydration and heat stress prevention. Avoid NSAIDs, nephrotoxins. Dialysis or transplant for ESRD. Prevention: occupational health measures.

distractors.explanation: Young, no DM, no HTN, minimal proteinuria, agricultural work.
No diabetes.

evidence.title: IDSA/ESCMID Guidelines: Acute Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis 2010
evidence.cite: Gupta K, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103-e120
points: CYSTITIS FIRST-LINE: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x 5 days (A-I); TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days if resistance <20% (A-I); Fosfomycin 3g single dose (A-I)
AVOID fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis - reserve for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI due to collateral damage (A-III)
Beta-lactams (amoxicillin-clavulanate, cephalosporins) are ALTERNATIVES only - inferior efficacy vs other agents (B-I)
Amoxicillin/ampicillin should NOT be used for empiric treatment - high resistance rates (A-III)

illnessScript.epidemiology: 50-60% of women will have UTI in lifetime. Risk factors: female sex, sexual activity, spermicide use, prior UTI, post-menopause, diabetes, urinary catheter. E. coli causes 75-95% of uncomplicated cases.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Uropathogenic bacteria ascend from periurethral area → bladder colonization → epithelial invasion → inflammatory response → mucosal edema and irritation → dysuria, frequency, urgency. Complicated UTI involves structural/functional abnormality or resistant organisms.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 118/74, HR 72, Temp 98.8°F - afebrile with normal vitals.; Well-appearing young woman, no acute distress.; Soft. Mild suprapubic tenderness. No rebound. No CVA tenderness.
illnessScript.workup: Uncomplicated: clinical diagnosis sufficient (dysuria + frequency without vaginal symptoms). Urinalysis if uncertain. Urine culture for complicated UTI, recurrent UTI, or treatment failure.
illnessScript.treatment: Uncomplicated cystitis: nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x5 days (first-line) OR TMP-SMX x3 days (if local resistance <20%) OR fosfomycin single dose. Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated UTI.

distractors.explanation: Must assess for fever, flank pain to rule out upper tract infection.
Sexually active - must consider chlamydia/gonorrhea.
Chronic symptoms, negative cultures - not this acute presentation.
